remediationRequest.name: rr-a661bc2c2d30-4445fab5
remediationRequest.outcomeNote: AA succeeded (93% confidence), WFE failed: scale-replicas-job amd64 binary crashed under QEMU on arm64 Kind node (lfstack.push panic)

rootCauseAnalysis.summary: Deployment web-cluster was manually scaled from 2 to 20 replicas, causing 5 pods to remain Pending because the cluster's 4 nodes (~38.7 GiB total allocatable memory) cannot satisfy the combined 40 GiB memory request (20 pods × 2 GiB each).
causalChain: Signal: KubePodSchedulingFailed — 5 pods in demo-autoscale stuck Pending with 'Insufficient memory' on all 4 nodes
Why? The scheduler cannot find a node with 2Gi free memory to place the pending pods — all nodes are at 83–89% memory request utilization
Why? 20 replicas of web-cluster are requested, each with a 2Gi memory request, totaling 40 GiB — exceeding the cluster's ~38.7 GiB total allocatable memory
Why? The Deployment was scaled from 2 to 20 replicas in a single kubectl scale operation at 21:21:39Z, with no HPA or admission control to prevent over-provisioning

Root cause: A direct manual kubectl scale command set replicas=20 on Deployment web-cluster without accounting for the cluster's memory capacity, and the per-pod memory request of 2Gi is significantly over-provisioned relative to actual usage (~300–600 MiB observed), making the 20-replica target unachievable on this 4-node cluster

dueDiligence.targetAccuracy: Targeting Deployment/web-cluster because its spec.replicas=20 is the direct cause of the over-scheduling. Reducing replicas to ≤15 (or reducing the memory request) will resolve the pending pods. The Deployment is the resource whose configuration change (replicas count or container resources) fixes the problem.
dueDiligence.proportionality: The remediation targets Deployment/web-cluster directly — the resource whose replica count caused the issue. Scaling down to ≤15 replicas or reducing the 2Gi memory request are both targeted fixes. No other workloads are contributing to the scheduling failure.
dueDiligence.scopeCompleteness: Investigated: all pods in namespace, Deployment spec and events, ReplicaSet, HPA (none found), all 4 nodes (capacity, conditions, actual usage), Prometheus memory request utilization per node. Did not investigate ResourceQuota/LimitRange (scheduler message does not cite quota), network policies (not relevant to scheduling), or PodDisruptionBudgets.
dueDiligence.causalCompleteness: Full causal chain traced: Pending pods → insufficient memory → 20 replicas × 2Gi exceeds cluster capacity → manual kubectl scale without capacity planning → over-provisioned memory requests. Cannot go deeper without access to the operator who ran the scale command.
dueDiligence.evidenceSufficiency: All claims backed by tool evidence: kubectl_get_by_kind_in_namespace confirmed 5 Pending pods with 'Insufficient memory'; Deployment events confirmed scale from 2→20 via kubectl; managedFields confirms manager=kubectl for replicas; HPA list is empty (no HPA); Prometheus confirms 83–89% memory utilization per node; kubectl_top_nodes confirms actual memory usage (303–647 MiB) far below 2Gi requests. No inaccessible data sources.
dueDiligence.regressionAwareness: No prior remediation history available for Deployment/web-cluster in demo-autoscale. This is the first recorded remediation attempt.
dueDiligence.alternativeHypotheses: Considered: (1) Node memory pressure/OOM — ruled out: kubectl_top_nodes shows actual memory usage is low (303–647 MiB on workers), no MemoryPressure condition on any node. (2) Node affinity/taint mismatch — ruled out: all 4 nodes have kubernaut.ai/managed=true label matching the nodeSelector, and scheduler message specifically cites 'Insufficient memory' not affinity. (3) HPA over-scaling — ruled out: no HPA exists in the namespace. (4) Resource quota — not investigated but scheduler message specifically calls out memory insufficiency, not quota exhaustion.
dueDiligence.confidenceCalibration: 0.95: Starting at 1.0, -0.03 for not verifying ResourceQuota/LimitRange (low probability given scheduler message), -0.02 for not having visibility into who/what triggered the kubectl scale command (manual vs. automation). All primary claims are fully evidence-backed.

contributingFactors: Each web-cluster pod requests 2Gi memory, which is a large request relative to node capacity (~9.68 GiB allocatable per node)
The Deployment was scaled from 2 to 20 replicas in a single step via kubectl scale (evidenced by managedFields manager=kubectl), creating 18 new pods simultaneously
No HPA exists — the scale was a direct manual operation with no guard against over-provisioning
All 4 nodes are now at 83–89% memory utilization with no remaining headroom for the 5 pending pods (each needing 2 GiB)
Pods have nodeSelector kubernaut.ai/managed=true which matches all 4 nodes, so the constraint is purely memory capacity, not node affinity
No preemption victims exist — no lower-priority pods can be evicted to make room

- **No `providerID`** set — not provisioned via the standard kind/Podman provider path; may be an externally joined node.

response_data.analysis: Deployment web-cluster was manually scaled from 2 to 20 replicas, causing 5 pods to remain Pending because the cluster's 4 nodes (~38.7 GiB total allocatable memory) cannot satisfy the combined 40 GiB memory request (20 pods × 2 GiB each).

rootCauseAnalysis.summary: Deployment web-cluster was manually scaled from 2 to 20 replicas, causing 5 pods to remain Pending because the cluster's 4 nodes (~38.7 GiB total allocatable memory) cannot satisfy the combined 40 GiB memory request (20 pods × 2 GiB each).
causalChain: Signal: KubePodSchedulingFailed — 5 pods in demo-autoscale stuck Pending with 'Insufficient memory' on all 4 nodes
Why? The scheduler cannot find a node with 2Gi free memory to place the pending pods — all nodes are at 83–89% memory request utilization
Why? 20 replicas of web-cluster are requested, each with a 2Gi memory request, totaling 40 GiB — exceeding the cluster's ~38.7 GiB total allocatable memory
Why? The Deployment was scaled from 2 to 20 replicas in a single kubectl scale operation at 21:21:39Z, with no HPA or admission control to prevent over-provisioning

Root cause: A direct manual kubectl scale command set replicas=20 on Deployment web-cluster without accounting for the cluster's memory capacity, and the per-pod memory request of 2Gi is significantly over-provisioned relative to actual usage (~300–600 MiB observed), making the 20-replica target unachievable on this 4-node cluster

dueDiligence.targetAccuracy: Targeting Deployment/web-cluster because its spec.replicas=20 is the direct cause of the over-scheduling. Reducing replicas to ≤15 (or reducing the memory request) will resolve the pending pods. The Deployment is the resource whose configuration change (replicas count or container resources) fixes the problem.
dueDiligence.proportionality: The remediation targets Deployment/web-cluster directly — the resource whose replica count caused the issue. Scaling down to ≤15 replicas or reducing the 2Gi memory request are both targeted fixes. No other workloads are contributing to the scheduling failure.
dueDiligence.scopeCompleteness: Investigated: all pods in namespace, Deployment spec and events, ReplicaSet, HPA (none found), all 4 nodes (capacity, conditions, actual usage), Prometheus memory request utilization per node. Did not investigate ResourceQuota/LimitRange (scheduler message does not cite quota), network policies (not relevant to scheduling), or PodDisruptionBudgets.
dueDiligence.causalCompleteness: Full causal chain traced: Pending pods → insufficient memory → 20 replicas × 2Gi exceeds cluster capacity → manual kubectl scale without capacity planning → over-provisioned memory requests. Cannot go deeper without access to the operator who ran the scale command.
dueDiligence.evidenceSufficiency: All claims backed by tool evidence: kubectl_get_by_kind_in_namespace confirmed 5 Pending pods with 'Insufficient memory'; Deployment events confirmed scale from 2→20 via kubectl; managedFields confirms manager=kubectl for replicas; HPA list is empty (no HPA); Prometheus confirms 83–89% memory utilization per node; kubectl_top_nodes confirms actual memory usage (303–647 MiB) far below 2Gi requests. No inaccessible data sources.
dueDiligence.regressionAwareness: No prior remediation history available for Deployment/web-cluster in demo-autoscale. This is the first recorded remediation attempt.
dueDiligence.alternativeHypotheses: Considered: (1) Node memory pressure/OOM — ruled out: kubectl_top_nodes shows actual memory usage is low (303–647 MiB on workers), no MemoryPressure condition on any node. (2) Node affinity/taint mismatch — ruled out: all 4 nodes have kubernaut.ai/managed=true label matching the nodeSelector, and scheduler message specifically cites 'Insufficient memory' not affinity. (3) HPA over-scaling — ruled out: no HPA exists in the namespace. (4) Resource quota — not investigated but scheduler message specifically calls out memory insufficiency, not quota exhaustion.
dueDiligence.confidenceCalibration: 0.95: Starting at 1.0, -0.03 for not verifying ResourceQuota/LimitRange (low probability given scheduler message), -0.02 for not having visibility into who/what triggered the kubectl scale command (manual vs. automation). All primary claims are fully evidence-backed.

contributingFactors: Each web-cluster pod requests 2Gi memory, which is a large request relative to node capacity (~9.68 GiB allocatable per node)
The Deployment was scaled from 2 to 20 replicas in a single step via kubectl scale (evidenced by managedFields manager=kubectl), creating 18 new pods simultaneously
No HPA exists — the scale was a direct manual operation with no guard against over-provisioning
All 4 nodes are now at 83–89% memory utilization with no remaining headroom for the 5 pending pods (each needing 2 GiB)
Pods have nodeSelector kubernaut.ai/managed=true which matches all 4 nodes, so the constraint is purely memory capacity, not node affinity
No preemption victims exist — no lower-priority pods can be evicted to make room

description.what: Expand a PersistentVolumeClaim by patching its storage request to a larger size, leveraging the CSI driver's online volume expansion capability.
description.whenToUse: When a PVC is projected to exhaust capacity based on storage growth trends (predict_linear), and the underlying StorageClass supports allowVolumeExpansion. The workload must be using a CSI-backed PVC with a filesystem that supports online resize (ext4, xfs).
description.whenNotToUse: When disk growth is caused by stale data that should be cleaned up rather than accommodated. When the StorageClass does not support volume expansion. When the PVC is backed by a non-CSI provisioner.
description.preconditions: The PVC's StorageClass has allowVolumeExpansion: true. The CSI driver supports ControllerExpandVolume and NodeExpandVolume RPCs. Sufficient storage capacity exists in the underlying storage pool.

description.what: Patches an OCP BuildConfig's source URI or ref to point to a valid Git repository, then triggers a new build.
description.whenToUse: Builds fail repeatedly because the BuildConfig references a non-existent Git repository, wrong branch, or unreachable URI.
description.whenNotToUse: When the build fails due to image builder errors, insufficient resources, or registry push failures.
description.preconditions: The correct Git URI and ref are known. The BuildConfig exists and the builder image is available.

description.what: Patches an OCP Route whose spec.to.name points to a non-existent or wrong Service, restoring external traffic flow.
description.whenToUse: An OCP Route returns 503/404 because spec.to.name references a Service that was renamed, deleted, or never created.
description.whenNotToUse: When the backend Service exists but the pods behind it are unhealthy. When TLS termination is misconfigured.
description.preconditions: The correct backend Service name is known. The Route exists and is owned by the target application.

description.what: Patches a Deployment's securityContext to comply with the assigned OCP SecurityContextConstraint, allowing pods to schedule.
description.whenToUse: Pods are blocked by SCC admission because the Deployment requests capabilities, runAsUser, or volume types not allowed by the SCC bound to the ServiceAccount.
description.whenNotToUse: When the SCC itself needs to be changed or when the workload genuinely requires privileged access.
description.preconditions: The target Deployment and namespace are known. The allowed SCC and its constraints are discoverable from the cluster.

description.what: Perform a graceful rolling restart of a workload to reset its runtime state.
description.whenToUse: Predictive analysis indicates an impending failure (memory leak, resource exhaustion) that can be prevented by proactively restarting before the crash occurs.
description.whenNotToUse: When the issue is caused by a code bug or misconfiguration that will recur after restart.
description.preconditions: Evidence of gradual resource degradation (trending metrics) and the workload supports rolling restarts without data loss.

description.what: Roll back a Helm release to its previous healthy revision.
description.whenToUse: A Helm-managed workload is crashing after a helm upgrade introduced a bad configuration, and the previous Helm revision was healthy.
description.whenNotToUse: When the workload is not managed by Helm (use RollbackDeployment or GracefulRestart instead), or when the crash is caused by an external dependency failure.
description.preconditions: The Helm release has at least one previous healthy revision in its history, and the Helm tiller/controller has access to roll back.

description.what: Increase memory resource limits on containers.
description.whenToUse: OOM kills are caused by memory limits being too low relative to the workload's requirements. This is the correct action for any ContainerOOMKilling signal, including suspected memory leaks -- increasing limits is the proper first-pass mitigation to buy runtime for investigation. The platform's escalation guardrails handle repeated ineffective attempts automatically.
description.whenNotToUse: When the deployment is constrained by a ResourceQuota -- increasing individual container limits does not increase the namespace's total allocation and may worsen it by requesting more memory per pod against a fixed quota ceiling.
description.preconditions: The deployment exists and containers have memory limits set. The node has sufficient allocatable memory for the increased limit.

description.what: Patch a faulty ConfigMap or configuration resource in-place to fix a configuration error without rolling back the deployment.
description.whenToUse: When the root cause is a specific, identifiable configuration error in a mounted ConfigMap, and the fix is deterministic. Prioritizes speed over safety by modifying live resources.
description.whenNotToUse: When the root cause is unclear, when multiple configuration resources may be affected, or when a full rollback to a known-good revision is preferred for safety.

description.what: Roll back a deployment to stop SLO error-budget burn caused by a bad deployment change.

description.preconditions: Confirmed that all schedulable nodes are at or near capacity and pending pods have resource requests that cannot be satisfied.

description.what: Recreates a missing or expired ImagePullSecret and restarts affected workloads to recover from ImagePullBackOff.
description.whenToUse: Pods are stuck in ImagePullBackOff because the referenced ImagePullSecret was deleted, expired, or contains invalid registry credentials.
description.whenNotToUse: When the image reference itself is wrong (tag does not exist, wrong registry URL). When the registry is rate-limiting pulls.
description.preconditions: The target namespace and Deployment name are known. A known-good credential template exists.

description.what: Temporarily relax a PodDisruptionBudget to unblock a pending node drain, then restore it.
description.whenToUse: A node drain is blocked because a PDB prevents evicting the required number of pods.
description.whenNotToUse: When the PDB is correctly preventing unsafe evictions and the drain should be postponed.
description.preconditions: Confirmed that the drain is intentional and the PDB is the sole blocker. The workload can tolerate temporary reduced availability.

description.what: Recreates a deleted or corrupted RoleBinding / ClusterRoleBinding so that the affected ServiceAccount regains its RBAC permissions.
description.whenToUse: A workload fails with 403 Forbidden because its RoleBinding was accidentally deleted or modified, removing required RBAC grants.
description.whenNotToUse: When the ServiceAccount itself is missing. When the ClusterRole referenced by the binding does not exist.
description.preconditions: The target namespace, ServiceAccount, and required Role/ClusterRole are known. The original binding spec is recoverable from the workflow definition.

description.what: Revert a deployment to its previous stable revision.
description.whenToUse: Root cause is a recent deployment that introduced a regression (bad image, broken command/args, misconfigured env vars), and the previous revision was healthy. Intended for stuck rollouts (progressDeadlineSeconds exceeded) and crashlooping pods caused by spec changes.
description.whenNotToUse: When the trigger is an SLO error-budget burn alert (ErrorBudgetBurn) or SLO-violation signal — use ProactiveRollback instead. Do not use when containers are OOMKilled (ContainerOOMKilling signal or OOMKilled termination reason) — OOMKill is a memory pressure problem, not a deployment regression. Rollback does not change memory limits; use IncreaseMemoryLimits instead.
description.preconditions: A previous healthy revision exists (verify via rollout history) and the issue started after the most recent deployment. The container must not be terminated with reason OOMKilled.

description.what: Horizontally scale a workload by adjusting the replica count.
description.whenToUse: Root cause is insufficient capacity to handle current load and the workload supports horizontal scaling.
description.preconditions: Evidence of increased incoming traffic or load correlating with the resource exhaustion.

response_data.analysis: Deployment web-cluster was manually scaled from 2 to 20 replicas in a single kubectl scale operation, causing 5 pods to remain Pending. The cluster's 4 nodes provide ~38.7 GiB total allocatable memory, but 20 pods × 2 GiB each = 40 GiB — exceeding total cluster capacity by ~1.3 GiB. All 4 nodes are at 83–89% memory utilization with no headroom for the 5 pending pods. No HPA exists and no preemption victims are available. The fix is to scale the Deployment back down to ≤15 replicas, which the cluster can accommodate.

rootCauseAnalysis.summary: Deployment web-cluster was manually scaled from 2 to 20 replicas in a single kubectl scale operation, causing 5 pods to remain Pending. The cluster's 4 nodes provide ~38.7 GiB total allocatable memory, but 20 pods × 2 GiB each = 40 GiB — exceeding total cluster capacity by ~1.3 GiB. All 4 nodes are at 83–89% memory utilization with no headroom for the 5 pending pods. No HPA exists and no preemption victims are available. The fix is to scale the Deployment back down to ≤15 replicas, which the cluster can accommodate.
causalChain: Signal: KubePodSchedulingFailed — 5 pods in demo-autoscale stuck Pending with 'Insufficient memory' on all 4 nodes
Why? The scheduler cannot find a node with 2Gi free memory to place the pending pods — all nodes are at 83–89% memory request utilization
Why? 20 replicas of web-cluster are requested, each with a 2Gi memory request, totaling 40 GiB — exceeding the cluster's ~38.7 GiB total allocatable memory
Why? The Deployment was scaled from 2 to 20 replicas in a single kubectl scale operation at 21:21:39Z, with no HPA or admission control to prevent over-provisioning

Root cause: A direct manual kubectl scale command set replicas=20 on Deployment web-cluster without accounting for the cluster's memory capacity, and the per-pod memory request of 2Gi is significantly over-provisioned relative to actual usage (~300–600 MiB observed), making the 20-replica target unachievable on this 4-node cluster

dueDiligence.targetAccuracy: Targeting Deployment/web-cluster because its spec.replicas=20 is the direct cause of the over-scheduling. Reducing replicas to ≤15 (or reducing the memory request) will resolve the pending pods. The Deployment is the resource whose configuration change (replicas count or container resources) fixes the problem.
dueDiligence.proportionality: The remediation targets Deployment/web-cluster directly — the resource whose replica count caused the issue. Scaling down to ≤15 replicas or reducing the 2Gi memory request are both targeted fixes. No other workloads are contributing to the scheduling failure.
dueDiligence.scopeCompleteness: Investigated: all pods in namespace, Deployment spec and events, ReplicaSet, HPA (none found), all 4 nodes (capacity, conditions, actual usage), Prometheus memory request utilization per node. Did not investigate ResourceQuota/LimitRange (scheduler message does not cite quota), network policies (not relevant to scheduling), or PodDisruptionBudgets.
dueDiligence.causalCompleteness: Full causal chain traced: Pending pods → insufficient memory → 20 replicas × 2Gi exceeds cluster capacity → manual kubectl scale without capacity planning → over-provisioned memory requests. Cannot go deeper without access to the operator who ran the scale command.
dueDiligence.evidenceSufficiency: All claims backed by tool evidence: kubectl_get_by_kind_in_namespace confirmed 5 Pending pods with 'Insufficient memory'; Deployment events confirmed scale from 2→20 via kubectl; managedFields confirms manager=kubectl for replicas; HPA list is empty (no HPA); Prometheus confirms 83–89% memory utilization per node; kubectl_top_nodes confirms actual memory usage (303–647 MiB) far below 2Gi requests. No inaccessible data sources.
dueDiligence.regressionAwareness: No prior remediation history available for Deployment/web-cluster in demo-autoscale. This is the first recorded remediation attempt.
dueDiligence.alternativeHypotheses: Considered: (1) Node memory pressure/OOM — ruled out: kubectl_top_nodes shows actual memory usage is low (303–647 MiB on workers), no MemoryPressure condition on any node. (2) Node affinity/taint mismatch — ruled out: all 4 nodes have kubernaut.ai/managed=true label matching the nodeSelector, and scheduler message specifically cites 'Insufficient memory' not affinity. (3) HPA over-scaling — ruled out: no HPA exists in the namespace. (4) Resource quota — not investigated but scheduler message specifically calls out memory insufficiency, not quota exhaustion.
dueDiligence.confidenceCalibration: 0.95: Starting at 1.0, -0.03 for not verifying ResourceQuota/LimitRange (low probability given scheduler message), -0.02 for not having visibility into who/what triggered the kubectl scale command (manual vs. automation). All primary claims are fully evidence-backed.

contributingFactors: Each web-cluster pod requests 2Gi memory — large relative to node capacity (~9.68 GiB allocatable per node)
Deployment was scaled from 2 to 20 replicas in a single step via kubectl scale, creating 18 new pods simultaneously
No HPA exists — the scale was a direct manual operation with no guard against over-provisioning
All 4 nodes are at 83–89% memory utilization with no remaining headroom for the 5 pending pods (each needing 2 GiB)
Pods have nodeSelector kubernaut.ai/managed=true which matches all 4 nodes — constraint is purely memory capacity, not node affinity
No preemption victims exist — no lower-priority pods can be evicted to make room

alternativeWorkflows.rationale: ProvisionNode was considered since pods are Pending due to insufficient cluster resources, but the root cause is an erroneous manual over-scale — not a genuine capacity need. Adding a node would accommodate the misconfigured replica count rather than fix the underlying problem, and carries higher cost and risk.
